title: Gerunds and Infinitives

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content:
In English, when two verbs appear together, the second verb takes a specific form. Some verbs are followed by the <gerund> (-ing form), others by the <infinitive> (to + verb), and some accept both. Learning which pattern each verb follows is essential for natural English.

For example:

• I enjoy <reading> books. (gerund)
• I want <to read> a book. (infinitive)

There is no simple rule to predict the pattern — you need to learn them. But there are useful groups and patterns that make it easier.

sections.title: Verbs Followed by Gerund (-ing)
sections.content:
These common verbs are always followed by the <gerund> (-ing form):

<Enjoy>: I enjoy cooking Italian food.
<Avoid>: She avoids eating late at night.
<Mind>: Do you mind waiting a few minutes?
<Suggest>: He suggested going to the cinema.
<Finish>: We finished cleaning the house.
<Practice>: They practice speaking English every day.
<Consider>: I'm considering changing jobs.
<Imagine>: Can you imagine living in another country?
<Keep>: She keeps forgetting her keys.
<Miss>: I miss seeing my old friends.
<Deny>: He denied stealing the money.
<Risk>: Don't risk losing your job.

A helpful memory trick: many of these verbs describe reactions to actions or ongoing situations. Understanding the correct <verb pattern> — whether a verb takes a gerund or infinitive — is essential for natural English.

sections.title: Verbs Followed by Infinitive (to + verb)
sections.content:
These common verbs are always followed by the <infinitive> (to + verb):

<Want>: I want to travel the world.
<Decide>: She decided to study medicine.
<Hope>: We hope to see you soon.
<Plan>: They plan to move next year.
<Agree>: He agreed to help us.
<Refuse>: She refused to answer the question.
<Learn>: I'm learning to drive.
<Offer>: He offered to carry my bags.
<Promise>: She promised to call me.
<Expect>: I expect to arrive by 6pm.
<Afford>: We can't afford to buy a new car.
<Pretend>: He pretended to be asleep.

A helpful pattern: many of these verbs express intentions, desires, or future-oriented actions.

sections.title: Verbs That Accept Both Forms
sections.content:
Some verbs can be followed by either the gerund or the infinitive with little or no change in meaning:

<Like>: I like swimming. / I like to swim.
<Love>: She loves dancing. / She loves to dance.
<Hate>: He hates waiting. / He hates to wait.
<Start>: It started raining. / It started to rain.
<Begin>: She began crying. / She began to cry.
<Continue>: They continued talking. / They continued to talk.
<Prefer>: I prefer walking. / I prefer to walk.

With these verbs, both forms are correct and natural. However, the gerund often sounds slightly more informal and the infinitive slightly more formal.

sections.title: Prepositions + Gerund
sections.content:
After prepositions, we always use the <gerund>, never the infinitive:

<Interested in>: She's interested in learning French.
<Good at>: He's good at solving problems.
<Tired of>: I'm tired of waiting.
<Instead of>: Instead of complaining, do something.
<Before>: Before leaving, check your bags.
<After>: After finishing work, I went home.
<Without>: He left without saying goodbye.
<Look forward to>: I look forward to meeting you.

Note: <look forward to> uses the gerund because <to> here is a preposition, not part of an infinitive. This is a common mistake for learners.

sections.title: Gerunds and Infinitives in Context
sections.content:
Example 1: JOB INTERVIEW
Interviewer: Why do you want <to work> here?
Candidate: I enjoy <working> with people and I'm considering <changing> my career path. I decided <to apply> because I hope <to grow> professionally.

Example 2: MAKING PLANS
A: I suggest <going> to the beach this weekend.
B: I'd prefer <to stay> home. I don't mind <relaxing> here.
A: OK, but I refuse <to spend> the whole weekend watching TV.

Example 3: DAILY LIFE
I finished <eating> breakfast and started <to get> ready for work. I avoid <driving> during rush hour, so I plan <to take> the bus. I keep <forgetting> my umbrella, so I need <to remember> it today.

sections.title: Summary
sections.content:
Key points to remember:


• Verbs like enjoy, avoid, mind, suggest, finish, consider are followed by the <gerund> (-ing).
• Verbs like want, decide, hope, plan, agree, refuse are followed by the <infinitive> (to + verb).
• Verbs like like, love, hate, start, begin, continue accept both forms with similar meaning.
• After prepositions (interested in, good at, tired of, look forward to), always use the <gerund>.
• <There is no universal rule> — learning the patterns through practice is the most effective approach.
• When in doubt, check a dictionary: it will show whether a verb takes -ing, to, or both.

learningObjectives: Identify verbs followed by gerund (-ing form)
Identify verbs followed by infinitive (to + verb)
Recognize verbs that accept both forms
Apply gerund and infinitive patterns in context

keyVocabulary.definition: The -ing form of a verb used as a noun (e.g., swimming, reading)
The base form of a verb with 'to' (e.g., to swim, to read)
The grammatical structure that follows a verb
